metadata.career_relevance: Spring Boot is the #1 Java framework in industry. 85% of Java job postings mention Spring Boot. Every company that runs Java on the backend — Flipkart, Razorpay, Zerodha, HDFC, PhonePe — runs Spring Boot. After this topic, you understand what Spring Boot is, how it works, and why it's so popular. Every remaining module builds on this foundation.

hook.what_you_will_build: By the end of this topic, you'll create a Spring Boot project, understand @SpringBootApplication and auto-configuration, use @Component/@Service with @Autowired, configure via application.properties, and run your first embedded Tomcat server.

concept_explanation.what_is_spring_boot:
**Spring Boot — convention over configuration:**

Spring Framework (2004): a powerful DI container, but required extensive XML configuration. Hard to set up.

Spring Boot (2014): built ON Spring. Adds:
- **Auto-configuration**: detects what's on the classpath and configures it automatically
- **Embedded server**: Tomcat is bundled — no separate server installation
- **Starter dependencies**: one dependency pulls in everything you need
- **Production-ready**: health checks, metrics, externalized config built-in

```
Spring Framework: the engine
Spring Boot: the car — same engine, but with seats, wheels, and a key
```

You still write Spring code (DI, MVC, JPA), but Spring Boot removes 90% of the setup.
concept_explanation.project_setup:
**Creating a Spring Boot project:**

**Option 1: Spring Initializr** (start.spring.io)
- Go to start.spring.io
- Choose: Maven, Java 21, add dependencies (Spring Web, Spring Data JPA, etc.)
- Download, unzip, open in IDE

**pom.xml minimal Spring Boot setup:**
```xml
<parent>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-parent</artifactId>
    <version>3.2.0</version>
</parent>

<dependencies>
    <!-- Spring MVC + Tomcat + Jackson: everything for a REST API -->
    <dependency>
        <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
        <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
    </dependency>
</dependencies>
```

One dependency pulls in: Spring MVC, embedded Tomcat, Jackson JSON, logging. That's the 'starter' concept.

**Run:** `mvn spring-boot:run` or run `main()` in your IDE.
concept_explanation.auto_configuration:
**Auto-configuration — how Spring Boot knows what to set up:**

When `spring-boot-starter-web` is on the classpath:
- Spring Boot detects: 'Jackson is here → configure ObjectMapper'
- 'spring-webmvc is here → configure DispatcherServlet'
- 'tomcat is here → start embedded Tomcat on port 8080'
- 'no DataSource configured → skip database setup'

You can override any auto-configuration in `application.properties`:
```properties
# src/main/resources/application.properties
server.port=9090                    # change from 8080
spring.application.name=my-app     # app name in logs
logging.level.root=INFO             # log level

# Custom property:
app.max-orders-per-day=100
```

Read custom properties in code:
```java
@Service
class OrderService {
    @Value("${app.max-orders-per-day}")
    private int maxOrdersPerDay;
    // maxOrdersPerDay = 100 at runtime
}
```

visual_aid.description: Three panels: (1) Spring Boot startup sequence: main() → SpringApplication.run() → ApplicationContext created → @ComponentScan finds all @Components → @Autowired wires dependencies → Auto-configuration reads classpath → Embedded Tomcat starts on :8080 → 'Started Application in 2.3s'. (2) Dependency injection diagram: @Service OrderService box with arrows from @Repository OrderRepo and @Service EmailSvc being injected. Label 'Spring creates all three and connects them'. (3) Auto-configuration decision tree: is spring-web on classpath? → configure Jackson, DispatcherServlet, Tomcat. Is spring-data-jpa on classpath? → configure EntityManagerFactory, DataSource. Each auto-config shows the property that overrides it.
visual_aid.alt_text: Spring Boot startup sequence, DI wiring diagram, and auto-configuration classpath detection

worked_example.scenario: Build a minimal product catalog Spring Boot application that demonstrates @SpringBootApplication, @Service with @Autowired, @Value from properties, and @PostConstruct initialization.
worked_example.problem_statement:
Create a Spring Boot app with:
1. `Application.java` — main class with @SpringBootApplication
2. `ProductCatalogService` — @Service that loads products, @PostConstruct init
3. `AppConfig` — @Value reads from application.properties
4. `application.properties` — port, name, custom max-products value
5. Demonstrate that Spring wires everything without manual instantiation

step_by_step_solution.details:
```properties
# src/main/resources/application.properties
server.port=8080
spring.application.name=product-catalog
logging.level.com.codequest=DEBUG

# Custom properties:
catalog.max-products=50
```

worked_example.expected_output:
.   ____          _            __ _ _
 /\\ / ___'_ __ _ _(_)_ __  __ _ \ \ \ \
...
Started Application in 2.341 seconds
Catalog loaded: 3 items (max: 50)
Products: [Wireless Mouse, Keyboard, USB Hub]
Max allowed: 50
things_to_notice: @SpringBootApplication on the main class triggers three things: @Configuration (this class can define beans), @EnableAutoConfiguration (spring-boot detects and configures everything), @ComponentScan (scans the current package and all sub-packages for @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller).
Spring creates ProductCatalogService automatically — you never call `new ProductCatalogService()`. When StartupRunner needs it, Spring injects the single shared instance.

@Value("${catalog.max-products:100}") reads from application.properties. The `:100` is the default if the property is not set. This is how you externalize configuration — change behavior without recompiling.

@PostConstruct on loadProducts() runs after Spring has created the bean AND injected all @Autowired/@Value fields. If you put initialization in the constructor, @Value fields aren't set yet — use @PostConstruct for initialization that needs injected values.

The single-constructor rule: if a class has exactly ONE constructor, @Autowired is optional — Spring injects it automatically. This is the modern preferred style: no @Autowired annotation on constructors.

industry_examples.use_case: Razorpay's payment APIs are Spring Boot applications. Each microservice is a separate @SpringBootApplication. The payment processing service has @Service beans for validation, authorization, and notification. @Value reads API keys and limits from environment variables (not hardcoded). Embedded Tomcat serves HTTP. Spring's DI wires all the components together — the same patterns you're learning now.

Flipkart's product catalog service is a Spring Boot microservice. ProductRepository (@Repository) handles database access. ProductService (@Service) implements business logic (inventory checks, price validation). ProductController (@RestController) handles HTTP. Spring Boot auto-configures HikariCP (connection pool), Jackson (JSON), and embedded Tomcat. One @SpringBootApplication annotation starts it all.

Almost every Java backend interview asks about Spring Boot. Common questions: 'What does @SpringBootApplication do?' (three annotations: @Configuration, @EnableAutoConfiguration, @ComponentScan), 'How does dependency injection work in Spring?' (IoC container creates and wires beans), 'What is auto-configuration?' (classpath detection → automatic setup). Understanding these is mandatory for any Java backend role.

applications.interview_relevance:
Spring Boot interview questions — must know:

- **'What is @SpringBootApplication?'** → @Configuration + @EnableAutoConfiguration + @ComponentScan in one annotation.
- **'What is dependency injection?'** → IoC pattern: instead of creating dependencies yourself, the container creates and injects them.
- **'What is the difference between @Component, @Service, @Repository, @Controller?'** → Functionally the same (all register beans). Semantic difference: @Service = business layer, @Repository = data layer + exception translation, @Controller = web layer.
- **'What is auto-configuration?'** → Spring Boot detects what's on the classpath and configures it automatically. Override with application.properties.
applications.real_world_workflow:
Daily Spring Boot development:

- New service class: annotate with @Service, inject dependencies via constructor
- New repository: annotate with @Repository, extends JpaRepository
- New property: add to application.properties, inject with @Value
- New profile: application-dev.properties vs application-prod.properties
- Run locally: `mvn spring-boot:run` or IDE play button

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: @Autowired on a field: `@Autowired private OrderService service;` — common but problematic.
common_gaps.remediation: Prefer constructor injection. Reasons: (1) immutability — final fields, (2) testability — pass mocks in constructor without Spring context, (3) Spring itself recommends constructor injection. For single-constructor classes, @Autowired is implicit — cleanest style.
common_gaps.detection_pattern: @Autowired directly on fields instead of constructor

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Placing @Service in a different package than Application.java — Spring doesn't find it.
common_gaps.remediation: @ComponentScan scans the package of the @SpringBootApplication class and all sub-packages. Put all classes in sub-packages of the main class package. Or use @SpringBootApplication(scanBasePackages = 'com.myapp').

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: ServiceA injects ServiceB, ServiceB injects ServiceA — circular dependency.
common_gaps.remediation: Spring Boot 2.6+ makes circular dependencies fail by default. Resolve by: extracting shared logic to a third service, using @Lazy on one injection, or restructuring responsibilities.

key_takeaways: SpringApplication.run(): creates ApplicationContext, auto-configures, starts Tomcat
Auto-configuration: Spring Boot reads classpath and configures everything automatically
Constructor injection: preferred way to inject dependencies
@Autowired: optional on single-constructor classes (Spring injects automatically)
@PostConstruct: initialization code after all dependencies are injected
application.properties: externalize all configuration (port, logging, custom)
Component scan: Spring finds all annotated classes in the main package and sub-packages
Singleton scope (default): one shared instance per bean

self_check_questions.question: You add @Service to a class but get 'NoSuchBeanDefinitionException' when it's @Autowired elsewhere. What's the most likely cause?
self_check_questions.answer: The @Service class is in a different package than the @SpringBootApplication class. @ComponentScan only scans the main class's package and sub-packages. Fix: move the @Service class into a sub-package of the main application package, OR add `@SpringBootApplication(scanBasePackages = 'com.myapp')` to explicitly specify the root package to scan.

self_check_questions.question: What's the difference between @PostConstruct and the constructor for initialization code?
self_check_questions.answer: The constructor runs BEFORE Spring injects @Autowired dependencies and @Value fields. If you initialize in the constructor, these fields are still null/default. @PostConstruct runs AFTER Spring has completed all injection — all @Autowired fields are set, all @Value fields are read from properties. Use @PostConstruct for initialization that needs injected values (e.g., loading data using an injected repository, or checking an @Value config limit).

self_check_questions.question: You have ProductService that needs OrderRepository, and OrderService that also needs ProductService. Is this a problem?
self_check_questions.answer: Potentially yes — if the graph continues and creates a cycle (A→B→A). In the given case, ProductService→OrderRepository and OrderService→ProductService doesn't create a cycle — they form a DAG. But if OrderRepository also injected OrderService, you'd have A→B, B→C, C→A, which is a circular dependency. Spring Boot 2.6+ throws BeanCurrentlyInCreationException by default. Fix by extracting shared state to a third class, or restructuring responsibilities.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: **REST Controllers** — now that Spring Boot is running, Topic 13.2 builds the HTTP layer. @RestController handles incoming requests. @GetMapping/@PostMapping/@PutMapping/@DeleteMapping map URLs to methods. @RequestBody deserializes JSON. @PathVariable reads URL segments. @RequestParam reads query strings. By the end of Topic 13.2, you've built a full CRUD REST API.
